generated_question: What are the key symptoms and management practices for root diseases in pea cultivation?
generated_answer: Root diseases in peas are among the most destructive problems affecting pea cultivation, causing significant yield losses and plant mortality. The primary root diseases affecting peas include root rot (caused by Fusarium oxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani). These soil-borne fungal pathogens attack the root system, compromising the plant's ability to absorb water and nutrients, ultimately leading to stunted growth, wilting, and plant death.   Disease Description and Symptoms: Root diseases in peas typically manifest through a combination of above-ground and below-ground symptoms. Above-ground symptoms include yellowing of lower leaves (chlorosis), stunted plant growth, wilting during hot periods despite adequate soil moisture, and premature plant death. Below-ground examination reveals the most characteristic symptoms: darkened, water-soaked roots that turn brown to black, root decay and rot, reduced root mass, and absence of healthy white root tips. In cases of Fusarium root rot, a reddish-brown discoloration of the vascular tissue extending into the stem base is observed. Seedling damping-off may occur in severe infections, where young plants collapse shortly after emergence. The severity of root diseases increases under conditions of poor drainage, heavy clay soils, continuous pea cropping, and soil temperatures between 15-28°C with high moisture levels.   Integrated Disease Management:  - Implement a crop rotation of at least 3-4 years with non-leguminous crops such as cereals, brassicas, or onions to reduce soil-borne pathogen populations.   - Select well-drained fields with good soil structure, avoiding low-lying areas prone to waterlogging.  - Improve soil drainage through proper land preparation, including raised bed cultivation.   - Use certified disease-free seeds from reliable sources and treat seeds before sowing.   - Practice deep summer plowing to expose soil pathogens to solar heat and desiccation.   - Maintain optimal plant spacing to ensure good air circulation and reduce humidity around the root zone.   - Remove and destroy infected plant debris immediately to prevent pathogen spread.   - Avoid excessive irrigation and implement drip or furrow irrigation methods rather than flood irrigation.   - Incorporate well-decomposed organic matter to improve soil health and beneficial microbial populations.   - Adjust sowing dates to avoid periods of high soil moisture and temperature favourable for pathogen development.  - Treat the seed with 15 g of Talc based formulation of Pseudomonas fluorescens per kg seed before sowing.
